title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Public Works Committee approved several key items including a transportation funding resolution, awarded a $126,900 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel, and directed staff to create enforcement language for sewer discharge violations with fines of $100/day. The committee also approved construction service proposals and intergovernmental agreements for multiple road projects.

agenda.item: Acknowledge April 2026 water and sewer permits
Water and sewer connection agreement - Radkkey Avenue

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a Local Road Improvement Grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Scoffield Avenue and Community Center Drive, originally a 2029 project that will likely move to 2028. The grant covers over 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project. Michael noted the village has received over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion also covered Weston Avenue repairs by Integrity Grading and Excavating for pavement depression issues, and upcoming Scoffield Avenue repairs by HOS Construction for manhole and concrete issues.

Staff reported road limits were removed April 29th with Marathon County. Work included mailbox and sign repairs, dewatering operations near Weston Avenue for about a week, spring curb sweeping, hot mix patching, storm damage cleanup from East Nick, inlet repairs before Birch road work, and signing installation on Scoffield Avenue. Culvert failures on Jones Street required installation of a third 18-inch culvert. Shop work included seasonal changeover of plow equipment and various vehicle maintenance.

Josh reported on water service repairs, well maintenance, and significant clear water infiltration into the sanitary sewer system. In April, 37 million gallons were pumped into the water system but 53-54 million gallons were treated at the wastewater plant - approximately 15 million gallons of clear water infiltration. Staff identified sump pumps illegally connected to sanitary sewers as a contributing factor. The committee discussed the upcoming INI study by In-Pipe Technologies to identify infiltration sources.

Michael presented the annual report showing net operating income of $482,911 for 2025, similar to 2024's $490,000. Water loss is at 6%. The utility value increased to nearly $34 million with $6.7 million in infrastructure additions. The utility has approximately $6.8 million in cash reserves and serves about 6,100 customers total - 5,250 in Weston, 830 in Rothschild. Motion to acknowledge passed unanimously.

Michael explained this League of Wisconsin Municipalities sample resolution supports long-term transportation funding solutions at the state level. The village has decreased public works budgets recently and grant funding helps one-time costs but not recurring maintenance expenses. Wisconsin is relatively affordable for automobile ownership compared to neighboring states. Motion to recommend village board adoption passed unanimously.

Michael reported King Gravel submitted the low bid at $4.23/ton for crushing approximately 30,000 tons of recycled material at the Ryan Street site, totaling $126,900. The village saves approximately $11/cubic yard compared to market prices, resulting in about $130,000 savings on the three 2026 projects. Motion to award to King Gravel passed unanimously.

Staff sought guidance on fining residents who discharge clear water (primarily sump pumps) into the sanitary sewer. Current code allows fines of $100-$1,000 per day. The committee discussed distinguishing between unknowing and willful violations. They agreed to start at $100/day after first warning. Staff will wait for INI study results, then send violation letters giving 30-60 days to correct before fines begin. Motion to direct staff to create enforcement language with $100/day fines passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Water and Sewer Connection - Radkkey Avenue
discussions.body: A duplex developer at 2230 Radkkey Avenue needs temporary water/sewer connection via long lateral until Radkkey Avenue is reconstructed. The committee added language requiring reconstruction no later than 2029, at which point the property owner will pay assessment for main extension. Motion passed unanimously with the 2029 deadline added.

The village will reimburse Rothschild for $94,860 in water and sewer work as part of Rothschild's Military Road project from Wolfman Street to Helin Street. Steam Construction is the contractor. The water main must be relocated due to storm sewer conflicts. Motion to approve the intergovernmental agreement passed unanimously.

Staff sought construction inspection services for two projects. For Alderson/Jelnik intersection (roundabout), MSA proposed $37,315 vs Clark Deetz at $48,910 - staff recommended MSA. For Bladel Avenue, MSA proposed $70,400 vs Clark Deetz at $73,730 - staff recommended Clark Deetz due to having full-time experienced staff rather than summer interns. Motion to approve MSA for roundabout and Clark Deetz for Bladel passed unanimously.

actionItems: Award 2026 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel for $126,900 (estimated 30,000 tons at $4.23/ton)
Forward Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding to village board for adoption
Staff to create enforcement language for sewer discharge violations with $100/day fines
Schedule special assessment meeting for Bladel Avenue project for May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM
Approve water/sewer connection agreement for 2230 Radkkey Avenue with reconstruction deadline no later than 2029
Approve intergovernmental agreement with Rothschild for Military Road construction at $94,860
Award Alderson/Jelnik construction services to MSA and Bladel Avenue construction services to Clark Deetz
Norcon to begin concrete repairs on Scoffield Avenue eastbound lanes starting Wednesday/Thursday
Staff to investigate street lighting options for Scoffield Avenue from Birch Street to Camp Phillips Road

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its first meeting after reorganization, electing new officers and approving a $5,000 grant for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission learned that a competing hot air balloon event in Marshfield was canceled, potentially increasing attendance at the Weston event.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from April 21st meeting
New business - Grant request for Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Remarks from staff and commission members

discussions.body: The commission elected new officers for the reorganized commission. Trustee Erling was nominated and elected as Chair unanimously. Cushing was nominated by a commissioner and seconded by Jackson, then elected as Vice Chair unanimously. Renee was nominated and elected as Secretary unanimously.
Minutes from the April 21st meeting were approved. Motion was made and seconded by Renee. Passed unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Grant Request - Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill of 3308 Green Pastures Lane presented the grant request. He reported estimated attendance of 11,000-12,000 last year and announced that the competing Marshfield hot air balloon event was canceled due to permit issues, making theirs the sole event in the area. A commissioner questioned room night tracking, noting the application showed 20 fewer rooms than last year when they received $5,000. The commission discussed needing better hotel room data for future decisions. Motion was made to approve $5,000 grant matching last year's amount, with a request for post-event reporting on attendance changes. Passed unanimously.

Renee reported on attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar. She described meeting the candidates who toured Marathon County attractions including Mullen's Cheese. She noted this was the first time Marathon County hosted the event since the 1970s. The winner, Anastasia, sat at their table and held three bachelor's degrees.

actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant to Taste and Glow Balloonfest for their annual event
Taste and Glow organizers to provide hotel room usage data in their post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM (tentative)

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved a site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and adopted a salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection areas. Both items passed unanimously with all members present voting in favor.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Creation of salt storage shed inspection program for Wellhead Protection District
April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
Planning and development department monthly project update report

discussions.item: Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive, noting it meets all B2 zoning requirements. Chief Jeremy explained challenges with a gas pipeline requiring the building to be rotated and positioned on the higher part of the site to reduce fill costs. The building will accommodate 50-60 officers for future growth beyond the current 40 officers. Discussion addressed an alternate storage shed location, with commissioners suggesting moving it further north to allow for future building expansion. The motion to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees with staff conditions 2-5 passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Salt storage shed inspection program for Wellhead Protection District
discussions.body: Staff proposed a twice-annual inspection program for salt storage facilities in wellhead protection areas - a warm weather inspection for structural integrity and a winter operational inspection. The program includes a standardized inspection worksheet similar to DOT requirements. Staff noted one existing facility near the Sternberg well has never gone through the wellhead protection conditional use process and commissioners agreed staff should reach out to bring them into compliance. The motion to approve the program with reporting back to both Plan Commission and Public Works Committee passed unanimously.
Minutes from the April 13, 2026 meeting were approved unanimously without discussion.

discussions.item: April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
discussions.body: Approved unanimously without discussion.
Approved unanimously without discussion.

discussions.item: Planning and development department monthly project update
discussions.body: Staff announced the first meeting of the joint Plan Commission-Village Board steering committee for the comprehensive plan update is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. Staff will send email notification and calendar invites to commissioners.

publicComment: Jim Penson of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via online connection. He urged the commission to exercise common sense regarding landscaping requirements for the police department project, referencing previous relaxed standards for the Green Heck Turner building to avoid excessive taxpayer expense. He also requested the commission require annual reporting on the salt shed inspection program to ensure accountability, noting some zoning complaints are years old.
actionItems: Recommend site plan approval for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility to Board of Trustees with staff conditions
Implement salt storage shed inspection program with twice-annual inspections in wellhead protection areas
Report inspection program findings back to Plan Commission and Public Works Committee next year
Reach out to existing salt storage facility near Sternberg well that has not gone through conditional use process
Schedule joint Plan Commission-Village Board steering committee meeting for comprehensive plan on June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Staff to send email and calendar invites for June 1st steering committee meeting
Electric vehicle charging station regulations to be addressed in fall agenda

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an educational orientation meeting for new board members, featuring presentations from the Health Department, Veteran Services, and Aging & Disability Resource Center. No policy votes were taken; the meeting focused on introducing department missions, budgets, and services to committee members.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call noting remote participation
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Discussion of operational functions required by statute
Review of standing committee ordinances, missions, duties and jurisdiction
Health Department presentation by Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka
Discussion of Health Department budget and emergency preparedness
Social Services Department overview by Administrator Leonard
Veteran Service Department presentation by VSO Aaron Galindo
ADRC of Central Wisconsin presentation by Executive Director Mike Ray

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavalli made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. The motion carried unanimously with all in favor.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented the department's mission of preventing disease, promoting health, and protecting from environmental hazards. She highlighted over 1,200 licenses overseen, a new homeless system response position, the Nurse Family Partnership program with first graduates, and public health vending machines stocked with free resources including tick kits. The department has 37.6 FTE with about half of its $5 million budget from tax levy. Administrator Leonard noted environmental health services are designed to be self-supporting through fees.

Supervisor Kremes asked about pandemic preparedness following news of a virus outbreak. Health Officer Suginaka confirmed the department has plans in place, conducts training exercises for measles response, and has a public health emergency preparedness planning body. She stated staff are highly trained from COVID experience and would reallocate resources if needed. Administrator Leonard noted that for major emergencies, state and federal funding typically becomes available.

Administrator Leonard presented on behalf of Director Christa who was unavailable. The department has nearly 140 FTE and a 2026 budget over $22 million with about $7 million from tax levy. He highlighted the successful Family Keys housing stability pilot program which addresses housing instability among child welfare and youth justice populations, noting the program substantively changed based on board feedback.

VSO Aaron Galindo reported his small department (3.0 FTE) had 8,149 interactions in 2025, up from 5,900 the prior year. He noted $44 million in annual benefits flow to local veterans, with $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025. Major initiatives include surviving spouse benefits outreach, housing stability partnerships with Center for Veterans Issues, and suicide prevention efforts. In 2025, Marathon County had 30 suicides, 7 of which were veterans. Supervisor Kremes asked about regional coordination; Galindo confirmed good working relationships with neighboring counties and explained state statute requires each county to have its own VSO.

Executive Director Mike Ray explained the ADRC serves four counties (Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, Wood) with about 60 FTE and 385-400 volunteers. The organization provides resource assistance, meals on wheels, caregiver support, dementia care, and is the entry point for publicly funded programs like Family Care and IRIS. He highlighted significant financial challenges with federal funding flat since 2024 while nutrition costs increased 40-45% over four years, forcing service reductions. Marathon County's contribution is $413,135 for 2026.
Vice Chair Rosenberg caught that the stated next meeting date of May 29th was incorrect. The committee confirmed the correct date is Wednesday, June 3rd at 3:00 PM.

actionItems: April 1st meeting minutes approved
North Central Healthcare to be scheduled for a separate full presentation before the committee
ADRC Executive Director Mike Ray to present annual report with 2025 data to the full board
Committee members to email Chair Boots and Administrator Leonard with items they want on future agendas
Chair Boots to bring strategic plan discussion items to upcoming meetings
Next meeting scheduled for Wednesday, June 3rd at 3:00 PM

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees, passed several ordinances including mobile food vendor licensing and e-bike regulations, and discussed ongoing challenges with the farmers market location at Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026 and made appointments to the CLIPS committee.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees (Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015)
Approval of minutes from previous meetings
Acknowledge reports from boards, committees, and commissions with chair updates
Consent agenda - license renewals and vouchers
Ordinances including mobile food vendor, nuisances, e-bikes, and transportation funding resolution
Discussion on farmers market location at Kennedy Park

discussions.item: Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees
discussions.body: Staff presented proposed increases to park and recreation impact fees from $300 to $761 for single family units, $250 to $563 for duplex units, and $250 to $495 for multifamily units, based on the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Jim Pitel spoke in public comment questioning the justification for the numbers without detailed operational cost data. Trustee Motti supported the increase citing population growth and fairness to existing residents. Ordinance 26-010 was approved with motion by Lope Sorrell, second by Zagami, passing unanimously. Resolution 2026-015 was approved with motion by Hermling, second by Clark, passing unanimously.

Chief Finkey explained the ordinance creates a cooperative inspection system where Safer and neighboring municipalities (Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, and others) will recognize each other's fire inspections for food trucks via a sticker system, eliminating the need for multiple inspections. Motion by Lope Sorrell, second by Zagami, passed unanimously.

The board debated amendments to the remote meeting attendance policy. Discussion focused on removing language requiring justification for health, safety, or welfare reasons, with trustees wanting a broader policy where chairs simply approve or deny requests without evaluating excuses. Trustee Beck moved to amend by striking sentences requiring specific justifications. The amendment passed with two no votes from Trustee Hermling and Trustee Motti. The original motion as amended also passed with the same two no votes.

President appointed Stephanie Daniels (currently on Finance and HR) and Goldsen Mua to the CLIPS committee. Motion by Hermling, second by Lopes, passed unanimously. The board also agreed to include committee applicant resumes in future meeting packets. Openings remain on Parks, Finance/HR, and CLIPS committees.

discussions.item: Farmers market location at Kennedy Park
discussions.body: Discussion-only item regarding the farmers market's new location after losing the Camp Phillips/Schofield corner site to development. Trustee Beck raised safety concerns about cars parking on grass areas. Renee explained history: the market grew from 25 stalls at the old municipal building to 42 at Camp Phillips to 53 current spots at Kennedy Park where vendors can keep their vehicles. Mock Miller Park was discussed but rejected due to poor visibility and distance concerns. The board discussed that if no suitable location can be found, the market may need to pause. Renee clarified the market operates at no loss due to grants covering tokens and reimbursements from EBT/Aspirus programs.

discussions.item: Engineering technician vacancy and outside contractors
discussions.body: In public comment, Jim Pitel criticized spending $108,000 on outside engineering consultants. Public Works Director Michael Dolan explained the engineering technician position has been advertised multiple times but the specialized skill set is scarce in central Wisconsin, with even consulting firms pulling staff from southeastern Wisconsin. The contracts on the agenda are for two projects starting in June.

Public Works Director Dolan reported on the speed limit issue raised previously. The DOT engineering study showed the road as 35 mph, but the 85th percentile speed study showed 45 mph. A second speed study is needed but was delayed due to construction. The matter will return to the board, possibly at the joint meeting.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of Deon 52nd Street spoke criticizing the $108,000 expenditure for outside engineering contractors, questioning why the engineering technician position remains unfilled, criticizing the process for committee appointments noting Lisa Beck was introduced at a Safer meeting before official board appointment, and requesting better documentation on Kennedy Park project funding and timeline. He also questioned the park impact fee increases during the public hearing, arguing the village lacks detailed operational cost data to justify specific numbers.
actionItems: Board of Review adjourned to June 15, 2026
Park and recreation impact fees increased per Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015
Mobile food vendor license ordinance (26-008) approved creating regional inspection reciprocity
Ordinance 26-009 amending standing committees approved
Ordinance 26-011 amending nuisances chapter approved
Ordinance 26-012 amending bicycle regulations for e-bikes approved
Resolution 2026-014 supporting long-term transportation funding approved
Remote meeting attendance policy amended to remove justification requirements
Stephanie Daniels and Goldsen Mua appointed to CLIPS committee
Secondhand article and jewelry dealer license approved for Schofield Coin and Hobby LLC contingent on background check and occupancy permit
Compliance assurance plan approved
Committee applicant resumes to be included in future board packets
Comprehensive Plan Steering Committee first meeting scheduled for Monday, June 1 at 6:00 PM
Speed limit study for Weston Avenue to return to board after completion
Intergovernmental service agreement for building inspection assistance to Ringle Township to be developed

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board held an adjourned organizational meeting where committee appointments were approved, two at-large executive committee members were elected (Supervisors Hapa and Arstead), and a resolution was passed unanimously to reduce landfill tipping fees for storm debris following severe weather that devastated parts of the county, particularly the town of Ringle. The board heard public comment from flood-affected residents requesting fee waivers.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Election of at-large executive committee members
Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution 26-26: Landfill tipping fees modification for storm debris

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs presented committee appointments developed with Vice Chair Boots, emphasizing goals of increasing diverse perspectives, developing new supervisors as leaders, and honoring supervisor preferences. Over 34% of the board is in their first or second term. The motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims, was carried but not unanimous. Both county board chair and administrator appointments were included in one motion.

discussions.item: Election of at-large executive committee members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Vice Chair Boots). Supervisor Rosenberg explained she nominated Supervisor Leur for advocating for the county's youngest children and oldest elders. Paper ballots were distributed and supervisors selected two names. Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the executive committee.
Items C10 through C14A were approved on motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase
discussions.body: Motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.
Motion by Supervisor P, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

Administrator Leonard and Solid Waste Director Hagen Busher presented a proposal to reduce tipping fees for storm debris following severe weather that particularly impacted Ringle. The state waived $13.10 per ton, Ringle waived $2.33, and the county proposed reducing fees to $36.94 per ton (essentially covering costs). About 400 tons of debris was already processed. Two residents, Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck, were granted special permission to speak, requesting the county waive fees entirely for affected residents. Multiple supervisors asked questions about fee structures, timelines, and policy development. The board first voted unanimously to suspend rules to consider the resolution without standing committee review, then approved Resolution 26-26 unanimously on motion by Supervisor Arstead, seconded by Supervisor Morash.

publicComment: Two residents were granted special dispensation to speak on the tipping fee resolution. Jessica Toasty requested the county waive fees entirely, noting the state, DNR, and Ringle were waiving their portions, and asked for a six-month extension rather than 60 days. She stated Marathon County provided limited financial assistance and that one business alone had $40,000 in hauling expenses helping the community. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of residents whose homes must be demolished, noting some are underinsured and insurance adjusters haven't arrived yet for some of the approximately 44 affected homes.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved as presented by Chair Gibbs
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to at-large executive committee positions
Resolution 24-26 approved for out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution 25-26 approved for social services budget amendment
Resolution 26-26 approved reducing landfill tipping fees to approximately $36.94 per ton for certified storm debris through June 17th
Administrator to work with Community Foundation on potential reimbursement mechanism for uninsured debris disposal costs
Solid waste management board to develop clear policy within six months for handling similar situations in the future
Supervisors to be notified of future solid waste board meetings on this topic

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions unanimously and voted to forward a modified fee waiver resolution to the county board that will only waive reconnect fees for septic systems (not other zoning or POWTS fees) for properties damaged by the April 17, 2026 tornado. The committee also received educational presentations from Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning departments.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing: Tim Veland rezoning petition (urban residential/general agriculture to rural residential) - Town of Green Valley
Committee deliberation and vote on Green Valley rezoning
Public hearing: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture) - Town of McMillan
Consideration of fee waiver for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Educational presentations: Solid Waste Department overview
Educational presentations: Parks, Recreation and Forestry overview
Educational presentations: Conservation Planning and Zoning overview

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Tim Veland rezoning petition - Town of Green Valley
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle, Land Use Specialist, presented the staff report for rezoning from urban residential and general agriculture to rural residential for property owned by James Offer and David Tract. The Town of Green Valley recommended approval on March 10, 2026, and CPZ staff recommended approval. No public comment was offered. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact and town approval, seconded by Supervisor Kurt. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition - Town of McMillan
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the petition to rezone from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy nature and potential loss of natural areas. Pogle explained the property contains a home and horse riding stable, and rezoning would make the home a conforming use rather than non-conforming. No natural areas would be lost. Town of McMillan recommended approval April 20, 2026. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve, seconded by Supervisor Drew. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waiver for April 17, 2026 severe weather victims
discussions.body: Director Lori Mskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and POWTS fees for properties damaged by the tornado affecting towns of Ringle, Eastston, villages of Kronenwetter and Weston. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future events. Supervisor Sefelt asked about potential levy impacts. Marilyn Bend asked about septic reconnection costs. After discussion, Supervisor Sefeld moved to forward the resolution. Supervisor Drebeck amended the motion to only waive reconnect fees ($160) for POWTS systems, not other zoning or POWTS fees. The amendment was seconded by Supervisor Kurt and passed. The amended resolution to forward to county board passed, though not unanimously.

Director Dave Hagen Busher presented an overview of the department's operations, including the landfill history since 1980, the renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy generating royalties for the county, and the upcoming $6 million expansion project. He highlighted challenges with leachate treatment due to PFAS regulations requiring on-site treatment by 2032, and lithium-ion battery fires occurring about once weekly.

Tom Lovelin, County Forest Administrator, presented the department overview covering 17 county parks, 30,700 acres of county forest, and services provided to the City of Wausau. He noted Nine Mile County Forest generates $3.25 million for the local economy from out-of-town visitors. Supervisor Sefeld announced Lovelin is retiring June 1st after nearly 30 years of service.

actionItems: Forward amended resolution to county board waiving only POWTS reconnect fees ($160) for properties damaged by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Rezoning for James Offer and David Tract property forwarded to county board for approval
Rezoning for Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz property forwarded to county board for approval
CPZ to track costs and requests related to tornado fee waivers for future policy development
County to reapply for Clean Water Fund grant for leachate treatment facility by October 2025 for 2027 funding

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks Committee deferred discussion on allowing dogs at the aquatic center to next month, approved installation of new kayak launch signs along the Plover River, and received updates on Kennedy Park development and Yellow Banks disc golf course improvements. Public commenters expressed opposition to dogs at the pool and criticized the 4 p.m. meeting time and Kennedy Park fundraising transparency.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog Day at the aquatic center
Graffiti on the Oaklair River Trail

discussions.body: Minutes from the April 27, 2026 meeting were approved. A motion was made and seconded with no discussion. The motion carried unanimously.

Director Sean Osterbrink presented a timeline of the Kennedy Park project from the comprehensive outdoor recreation plan approval in November 2023 through the recent ribbon cutting on May 9th. Scott asked about village capital dollars earmarked for Kennedy Park; Osterbrink confirmed there are currently no village funds earmarked as those items were pulled due to the Kennedy Park project. The Friends of Kennedy Park are fundraising for lights on the upper field, currently at approximately $84-85,000 toward a $117,000 goal. The committee discussed that the Friends group plans to make a public check presentation at their first tournament in June. Committee members emphasized giving the volunteer Friends group grace and noted master plans are guides that can change over time.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Staff presented photos and a PDF showing the approximate location of trees removed from the river and the installation of wood mats over gas pipelines to protect them during equipment access. The mats are 8x8 posts with notches and lifting rings. TC Energy (formerly ATC/ANR pipeline) visited the site and conducted a walkthrough after the work was completed. The village has a couple extra mats that may be used on another trail project.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the aquatic center
discussions.body: The committee deferred this item to next month because Chair Katrina, who had requested the discussion, was absent due to an unforeseen issue. A member of the public who came specifically to oppose dogs at the pool was told to return next month. No formal motion was required for the deferral.

The committee discussed challenges removing graffiti from concrete and benches along the trail. Staff explained they cannot get pressure washing equipment to the trail due to steep ditches and narrow bridges. Options discussed included anti-vandalism coatings, seal coating, and potentially organizing volunteer work days. Committee member Laro suggested community volunteers might help, noting people love the trail. Staff mentioned the memorial benches are 2,000 lbs each. No formal action was taken; staff will investigate cleaning options.

Dan Gleason from Marathon County (2625 Northwestern Avenue) presented version 7 of signs for Plover River kayak launches. Three signs will be installed in Village of Weston parks (Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks). The signs include a map, QR code for river gauge site, and donation URL for Marathon County Parks Foundation. Gleason noted the Highway J launch will likely be closed all season due to bridge replacement. A motion to approve installation of the signs was made, seconded, and passed unanimously.

Staff raised the possibility of food allergy awareness signage on playgrounds, noting an organization offered to provide free signs. The committee agreed to bring it back for further discussion. The Mock Miller park master plan process will begin with a kickoff meeting with Rettler, and the last bike and pedestrian plan open house is June 11th from 5-7 p.m.

publicComment: Two public comments were received. Andy (6504 [address inaudible]) spoke in person opposing dogs at the aquatic center, stating 'There should not be animals allowed into a public pool. Period.' Jim Pensan (5002 [Street] Weston), speaking online under the name 'Vern M', criticized the 4 p.m. meeting time, questioned Kennedy Park funding transparency, and called the $8,700 expenditure figure 'too coincidental.' He asked where the $10,000 grant money, glow event funds, and lighting fundraising money are, calling the numbers 'convoluted and misleading.'
actionItems: Dog Day at aquatic center discussion deferred to June meeting
Kayak launch signs approved for installation at Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks - forwarded to Village Board
Staff to investigate graffiti removal and cleaning options for Oaklair River Trail
Food allergy awareness playground signage to be brought back for future discussion
Mock Miller master plan kickoff meeting with Rettler scheduled for next Tuesday
Next meeting scheduled for June 22, 2026

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee approved previous meeting minutes and acknowledged April financial reports. The meeting featured an extensive educational presentation from Municipal Clerk Pam on the clerk's office operations, covering elections, licensing, tax collection, and the quarterly newsletter. Committee member Evan announced his resignation as he is moving away from Weston.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
TIF 1 and TIF 2 detail reports for April
Educational presentation - Municipal Clerk's budget and office overview
Old business, new business, staff remarks

discussions.body: Motion to approve minutes was made by Ernling, seconded by Daniels. The motion carried with no opposition.

Discussion focused on TIF 1 final numbers and bond payoff timeline. Staff indicated the bonds are due October 1st and they anticipate calling the bonds in October/November. A resolution will need to go through the CDA this summer. Staff confirmed the village will be able to pay off the debt as projected.
The April budget status report, TIF detail reports, and legal details report were acknowledged together. Motion to acknowledge was made by Daniels, seconded by Soaper. Motion carried.

Clerk Pam presented an extensive overview of the clerk's office operations. The office has moved from four full-time staff to two full-time and one part-time employee. They recently added part-time workers instead of a third full-time position. Discussion covered elections (including Badger Book electronic poll books at $11,000 for four units), liquor licensing (51 total licenses), pet licensing ($17,480 collected in 2026 through March 31st), tax collection (84% done through clerk's office walk-ins totaling $22 million), and the quarterly newsletter (costing approximately $30,000 annually for postage and printing to 5,089 postal patrons). Pet licensing fees increased from $10 to $20 due to Marathon County raising fees to cover Humane Society costs.

Staff member Renee reported the farmers market at Kennedy Park has been going well. She has been present for 6-7 hours each Saturday starting at 6:00 AM. The market is full with food trucks, coffee vendors, craft vendors, and some early produce. Vendors reported being very happy with the setup and location. Some discussion occurred about parking concerns on Alderson Street and Newport, with staff noting that only the east side of Alderson is signed no parking.

Evan announced he is stepping down from the committee as he and his wife are moving away from Weston, though staying in the metro area. He thanked the committee for his time serving. The chair noted there are now open spots on this and other committees and encouraged outreach to citizens interested in participating.

actionItems: CDA resolution needed this summer to call TIF bonds due October 1st
Clerk's office seeking to hire one more part-time employee
Chief inspector wage increase to $15/hour to be proposed for next election term
Recruit citizens for open committee positions
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30 PM

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee recommended approval of nuisance ordinance amendments and e-bike regulations (with emoto restrictions), discussed winter overnight parking regulations without action, approved a crosswalk request contingent on Mount Olive Church paying costs, adopted mobile food vendor fire inspection requirements, and authorized demolition of an abandoned structure at 4104 Shore Avenue.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Acknowledgment of department reports (Code Enforcement, Fire/EMS, Police)
Discussion on proposed amendments to Chapter 50 Nuisances
Discussion on e-bike ordinance Chapter 82 amendments
Discussion on overnight parking regulations October 31 to May 1
Discussion on crosswalk request for Alderson Street
Discussion on mobile food vendor fire inspection requirements

discussions.body: Committee reviewed final language for nuisance ordinance amendments, including previously discussed tree limb height requirements of 14 and 10 feet over streets and walkways. Brent Tolson confirmed no additional changes were needed. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees. Passed unanimously.

Committee discussed proposed e-bike regulations based on a model ordinance. Chief Hunt expressed strong opposition to allowing emotos (electric mini-motorcycles) on roadways, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes and citing safety concerns. Discussion included whether to also restrict electric scooters, but committee opted to leave enforcement discretion to officers. Committee member Sherwanka questioned if licensed electric vehicles needed clarification. Motion by Olsen to recommend approval with addition prohibiting emotos on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property, seconded by Sherwanka. Passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Overnight parking regulations October 31 to May 1
discussions.body: Brent Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour parking restriction (4am-4pm) is significantly longer than surrounding municipalities, most of which have 4-5 hour windows (typically 2am-7am). DPW Director Michael Wodalski indicated he could support eliminating even/odd parking and just signing 6-8 specific problem areas. Chief Hunt opposed eliminating overnight restrictions entirely, citing need for time to clear vehicles before plowing. Committee discussed various approaches but took no action, directing Michael Wodalski to prepare specific exception locations for July meeting.

discussions.item: Crosswalk request for Alderson Street between Mount Olive Church and DC Everest
discussions.body: Request for crosswalk to connect church parking to Mount Olive Church across Alderson Street. DPW Director Wodalski estimated cost at approximately $2,000 for basic crosswalk or $25,000-30,000 for lighted system. Committee expressed reluctance to set precedent of paying for crosswalks upon request. Chief Hunt noted no safety incidents in the area. Motion by Olsen to recommend board approval contingent on Mount Olive Church covering installation costs, seconded by Sherwanka. Passed unanimously. Wodalski will contact the church.

discussions.item: Mobile food vendor fire inspection requirements
discussions.body: Fire Chief Hunt explained new regional fire inspection program involving Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Rib Mountain, and potentially Weston. Inspections are free, and trucks receive stickers valid across all participating municipalities. Chief noted one recent inspection failed due to improper LP piping and unsecured propane tanks. Committee member asked about Wausau's non-participation; Hunt stated they prefer doing their own inspections. Motion by Sherwanka to approve Ordinance 26-8, seconded by Campkey. Passed unanimously.

Staff presented three addressing errors: 5915 Delicoski Street (duplex needing separate addresses), Ross Avenue properties out of sequence, and 2211 Radkey Avenue apartment complex. Chief Hunt also identified Weston Villa Apartments on Delicoski as having improper addressing with eight apartments having separate addresses. Motion to recommend approval to Board of Trustees, seconded by Campkey. Passed unanimously.

Staff presented raze order for abandoned structure, vacant since 2010-2014 and posted for demolition since 2021. Previous fire training burn was cancelled due to unsafe structural conditions. Staff noted owners had housed chickens in the structure. Property is currently for sale. Owners are current on taxes and have agreed to demolition. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, to recommend board authorization to begin raze order. Passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke regarding several agenda items. He criticized the new farmers market location at the park, predicting grass destruction from vehicle parking. He raised concerns about ADA compliance for handicap parking spaces placed on grass rather than asphalt. He questioned the origin of new fire inspection requirements for mobile food vendors. He noted ambiguity in food truck ordinance language regarding farmers market days and suggested code violations have been pending too long without visible progress, specifically mentioning items that have been on the enforcement list for extended periods.
actionItems: Recommend approval of Chapter 50 Nuisance amendments to Board of Trustees
Recommend approval of e-bike ordinance with emoto prohibition on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property to Board of Trustees
DPW Director Wodalski to identify 6-8 specific locations for potential even/odd parking restrictions and present at July meeting
DPW Director Wodalski to contact Mount Olive Church regarding crosswalk cost-sharing
Recommend approval of Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspections
Recommend approval of E911 address corrections to Board of Trustees
Recommend authorization for staff to proceed with raze order on 4104 Shore Avenue
Staff to investigate property at Sternberg and South Timber regarding outdoor furniture and dumpster
3020 Mountain View Avenue to be removed from code enforcement roll following property sale and permit submission

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

agenda.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling (Action Requested)
Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on Employee Handbook Updates for 2026-27
Board was expected to vote on the Preliminary 2026-27 Budget recommendation
Board was expected to vote on 2026-2027 Paid Meal Price Update
Board was expected to vote on Activities & Athletics Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling revisions
Board was expected to vote on Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Riverview RFP Approval for HVAC and roofing project

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the 2026-27 Budget Reconciliation Plan
Board was expected to vote on 2026-27 Teacher Contract Approvals
Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff
Board was expected to vote on WAMCS Charter School Contract Renewal for 2026-2031
Board was expected to potentially take action following closed session regarding non-renewal notices and contract evaluations

The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Week of the Young Child Proclamation
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.
